questions.question: What are the primary objectives of this project (e.g., increase sales, improve UX, launch a new feature)?
questions.description: Defines the "why" behind the project. Clear objectives align all team members and prevent scope creep throughout development.

questions.question: Who are the target users or customers? What are their key demographics and needs?
questions.description: Understanding your audience shapes every design and technical decision — from font size to API performance requirements.

questions.question: What business metrics or KPIs will define success (e.g., conversion rate, retention, load times)?
questions.description: Specific, measurable KPIs keep the project accountable and help prioritize features that move the needle.

questions.question: Is this a new (greenfield) project or an update/migration of an existing system?
questions.description: Greenfield projects have more freedom in architecture; migrations come with constraints (existing data, integrations, URLs) that must be planned for.

questions.question: If migrating: What are the main reasons for the migration, and what must be preserved or improved from the current version?
questions.description: Migration projects risk losing critical functionality if not documented. List what must stay, what should improve, and what can be cut.

questions.question: Are there existing brand guidelines or style guides (colors, fonts, logos) to follow?
questions.description: Having brand guidelines upfront avoids redesign cycles. Even a basic brand sheet (colors, fonts, logo sizes) is enough to start.

questions.question: What overall visual style or tone should the UI convey (e.g., corporate, playful, minimalist)?
questions.description: Tone guides micro-decisions in typography, spacing, animation speed, and language. "Corporate" means different things to different clients.

questions.question: Do you have reference designs or competitor sites we should consider or avoid?
questions.description: Knowing what to emulate (or avoid) saves discovery time and aligns expectations before any design work begins.

questions.question: Will completed design mockups (e.g., Figma files) be provided, or is front-end expected to convert designs into code?
questions.description: Pixel-perfect Figma handoffs and "build from scratch" are very different scopes. Clarify this early to scope effort accurately.

questions.question: How should key user flows work (e.g., signup, checkout)? Are there mockups or prototypes for these flows?
questions.description: Key user flows are the most important things to get right. Mockups or prototypes here are worth more than a hundred words of description.

questions.question: Are there any mandated design systems or component libraries to use?
questions.description: Mandated libraries (e.g., MUI, Ant Design) affect bundle size, theming approach, and how much custom CSS is needed.

questions.question: Who is responsible for final approval of UI/UX (product owner, marketing team, etc.)?
questions.description: Without a clear approval owner, reviews loop endlessly. One named decision-maker keeps the process moving.

questions.question: How should we handle error states, empty states, and loading states in the UI? (e.g., any guidelines for spinners or placeholders)
questions.description: Empty/error/loading states are often missed in initial designs but are critical to a polished UX. Define them early.

questions.question: Are there specific animations or transitions (hover effects, modal popups) required, and should they be adjustable or toggleable (reduced motion)?
questions.description: Animations can be delightful or disruptive. Confirm specs for duration, easing, and whether reduced-motion users should see a fallback.

questions.question: Will designers provide final copy/text, or should we use placeholders until content is ready?
questions.description: Placeholder copy affects layout significantly. Know whether real content is coming before building flexible text-overflow handling.

questions.question: Who are the end users (general public, internal users, children, etc.)? Any special needs (e.g., accessibility requirements)?
questions.description: Different users have vastly different needs. Internal tools, consumer apps, and children's platforms each require tailored accessibility strategies.

questions.question: Are there legal or industry accessibility requirements (WCAG 2.1 level AA, ADA compliance, etc.)?
questions.description: WCAG 2.1 AA is the baseline for most public-facing sites. Some industries (healthcare, government) require AAA or specific regulations.

questions.question: What accessibility features are mandatory (keyboard navigation, screen reader support)?
questions.description: Keyboard navigation and screen reader support are non-negotiable for most accessibility standards. Confirm scope and testing requirements.

questions.question: Have we conducted or planned an accessibility audit? Who will do it?
questions.description: Audits catch issues that developers miss. Agree on timing (before launch? after?) and who conducts it (internal QA, third-party tool).

questions.question: Are color contrast and font sizes designed with accessibility in mind? (For example, WCAG recommends a 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text[1].)
questions.description: WCAG recommends 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text and 3:1 for large text. This affects color palette decisions significantly.

questions.question: Are alt text descriptions provided for all images and meaningful icons (improves accessibility and SEO[2])?
questions.description: Missing alt text fails automated accessibility checks and hurts SEO. Confirm who writes alt text — developer, designer, or content team.

questions.question: Should animations be disabled or reduced for users who prefer reduced motion?
questions.description: Users with vestibular disorders can be harmed by parallax or looping animations. The prefers-reduced-motion CSS media query handles this.

questions.question: Who on the team will verify accessibility compliance (UX specialist, QA)?
questions.description: Accessibility often falls through the cracks when no one owns it. Assign a responsible person before development starts.

questions.question: What are the core features and user actions (e.g., user registration, search, shopping cart)? List all key functionality.
questions.description: A complete feature list prevents mid-sprint surprises. Even rough bullet points are better than discovering missed features in QA.

questions.question: Are there any complex UI components required (e.g., data tables, charts, drag-and-drop, rich text editor)?
questions.description: Complex components (drag-and-drop, rich text editors, virtual scroll tables) require significantly more time than simple ones. Flag them early.

questions.question: For e-commerce: which payment gateways, shipping options, and tax rules are needed?
questions.description: Payment gateway integration can take weeks depending on the provider. Each gateway has its own checkout flow, redirect logic, and webhook setup.

questions.question: Do we need social logins or external authentication (OAuth providers like Google, Facebook)?
questions.description: Social logins reduce registration friction but require OAuth app setup per provider. Each provider (Google, Facebook, Apple) has its own review process.

questions.question: Are there any background processes or real-time features (notifications, live updates)?
questions.description: Real-time features (WebSockets, SSE) require backend infrastructure changes and affect how the frontend manages state and reconnections.

questions.question: What are the priority features (must-have vs. nice-to-have)?
questions.description: A clear must-have vs. nice-to-have split protects the launch date. Everything tends to be "must-have" until you apply this filter.

questions.question: Will there be user roles or permissions affecting the UI (admin vs. regular user screens)?
questions.description: Role-based UI means conditional rendering throughout the app. Know the roles early to plan routing, guards, and component variants.

questions.question: How should client-side error handling work? For example, what user-friendly messages or retry options should appear on network/API errors?
questions.description: Client-side error handling is often an afterthought. Agree on patterns (toast notifications, inline messages, retry buttons) before building.

questions.question: Are there existing analytics or user behavior data that influence feature priorities?
questions.description: Existing analytics data reveals which features actually get used. It's often the most objective source of truth for prioritization.

questions.question: Will the site use a CMS for content (e.g., Adobe Experience Manager, Magento)?
questions.description: CMS integration fundamentally changes the data-fetching architecture. Whether content comes from AEM APIs, Magento GraphQL, or a headless CMS affects every page component.

questions.question: How is content organized (page templates, components)? Will front-end fetch data from CMS APIs?
questions.description: Page templates and component models define what the CMS editor can control. This affects frontend component granularity.

questions.question: Who will supply the initial content (text, images)? Is the content final or subject to change?
questions.description: If initial content isn't ready, you'll be building with Lorem Ipsum, which leads to layout surprises when real content arrives.

questions.question: Are there images or media with specific format/size requirements? (Consider optimization for web.)
questions.description: Web images should be compressed and appropriately sized. WebP format with fallback provides the best balance of quality and file size.

questions.question: If migrating: How will existing content be migrated to the new system? Will data feeds or scripts be needed?
questions.description: Content migrations are frequently underestimated. Old content rarely maps cleanly to new data models — budget time for transformation logic.

questions.question: Are there multi-language versions of content? If so, how are translations managed in the CMS?
questions.description: i18n in a CMS requires translation workflows, locale-specific URL structures, and sometimes different component content per locale.

questions.question: Who will update content after launch, and will they need special editing tools?
questions.description: CMS editors who aren't developers need an intuitive editing UI. If they need custom tools, that's a separate dev effort.

questions.question: Which backend services or APIs will the front-end call? (REST endpoints, GraphQL, WebSocket streams, etc.)
questions.description: A clear API inventory prevents frontend blockers. Without knowing what endpoints exist, the frontend can't be properly built or estimated.

questions.question: Is API documentation available (Swagger/OpenAPI specs, Postman collection)?
questions.description: Swagger/OpenAPI specs allow frontend developers to understand request/response shapes without relying on constant backend availability.

questions.question: Will any APIs require authentication (tokens, cookies, OAuth)? How is this handled?
questions.description: Auth strategy affects every API call. JWT tokens need refresh logic; cookies need CORS configuration; OAuth needs redirect handling.

questions.question: What data formats and structures do the APIs use (JSON schema)?
questions.description: Understanding the JSON schema helps design the data layer. Nested vs flat structures significantly affect how components receive and transform data.

questions.question: Are there rate limits, pagination, or throttling constraints on the APIs?
questions.description: Rate limits require client-side throttling or retry logic. Pagination affects how lists are displayed and loaded.

questions.question: How are errors returned by the API (error codes, messages)? How should the front-end display them?
questions.description: Inconsistent error formats from the API mean the frontend must handle each case differently. Standardizing early saves significant defensive coding.

questions.question: If integrating third-party services (e.g., payment, maps, marketing tools): What are the endpoint details, credentials, and workflows for each?
questions.description: Third-party integrations (Stripe, Google Maps, etc.) each have their own auth, SDK, and error model. Budget time per integration.

questions.question: For real-time features: what technologies are in use (WebSockets, Firebase)?
questions.description: WebSocket connections require reconnection logic, heartbeat handling, and state reconciliation — significantly more complex than REST.

questions.question: If migrating: Are we keeping existing APIs or building new ones? What changes should we anticipate?
questions.description: API changes during migration are a major risk. Understand what's staying, what's changing, and what deprecation timeline exists.

questions.question: Are there performance targets or budgets (e.g., Lighthouse scores, load times)?
questions.description: Without targets, performance is subjective. Lighthouse score of 90+, LCP under 2.5s, and CLS under 0.1 are industry-standard baselines.

questions.question: Will the site use server-side rendering (SSR) or static generation for faster initial loads and better SEO?
questions.description: SSR dramatically improves Time to First Byte and SEO. Static generation (SSG) is even faster for content that doesn't change per user.

questions.question: Are responsive images and lazy loading required for images and media to speed up page loads? (Implementing lazy loading can significantly improve page speed[3].)
questions.description: Lazy loading images below the fold can reduce initial page load by 50%+ on image-heavy pages.

questions.question: Will static assets (images, fonts, JS/CSS) be served via a CDN to reduce latency[3]?
questions.description: A CDN reduces latency by serving assets from edge nodes close to the user. Critical for global audiences.

questions.question: What compression (gzip, Brotli) or bundling strategy is expected for assets?
questions.description: Brotli compression typically reduces text asset sizes 15–25% more than gzip. Tree-shaking and code splitting reduce bundle size.

questions.question: Is there a need for prefetching or caching strategies (e.g., HTTP caching headers, service workers)?
questions.description: Service workers enable offline support and background sync. HTTP caching headers prevent unnecessary re-downloads of unchanged assets.

questions.question: What is the expected traffic load and user concurrency? Are load-testing benchmarks required?
questions.description: Traffic estimates inform decisions about data fetching strategies, API caching, and whether client-side state can be kept in memory.

questions.question: How will performance be monitored (e.g., RUM tools, synthetic testing)?
questions.description: RUM (Real User Monitoring) tools like SpeedCurve or Datadog measure real-world performance, not just lab scores.

questions.question: Which pages need custom meta tags (titles, descriptions, keywords) for SEO? Who will provide the content?
questions.description: Each page ideally has a unique title and meta description. Without clear ownership, this gets skipped and SEO suffers.

questions.question: Will the site use semantic HTML and proper heading hierarchy for SEO?
questions.description: Semantic HTML (h1 hierarchy, nav landmarks, main content area) is foundational for both SEO and accessibility.

questions.question: Do we need to generate XML sitemaps or configure robots.txt for crawlers?
questions.description: An XML sitemap helps search engines discover all pages. robots.txt controls what gets indexed or excluded.

questions.question: If the site is a SPA (single-page app), how will search engines index it? (SSR or prerendering may be needed.)
questions.description: SPAs built without SSR/prerendering may not be indexed by Google. Verify the SEO strategy for client-rendered content.

questions.question: Should we implement Open Graph/Twitter Card metadata for social sharing?
questions.description: Open Graph tags control how links appear when shared on Twitter, LinkedIn, Slack, etc. — title, description, and preview image.

questions.question: Who is responsible for setting up analytics/tracking (Google Analytics, Google Tag Manager, etc.)?
questions.description: Analytics setup is often left until the last minute. Decide upfront who configures GTM, GA4, or other tools.

questions.question: What user actions or events should be tracked (purchases, sign-ups, form submissions)?
questions.description: Custom event tracking (button clicks, form submissions, video plays) must be specified before implementation — retrofitting is error-prone.

questions.question: Are cookies or tracking pixels subject to user consent (GDPR/CCPA)?
questions.description: GDPR/CCPA require explicit user consent before tracking cookies fire. Implementing consent banners is a feature in itself.

questions.question: How will we verify analytics integration in testing (e.g., test viewports, debug tools)?
questions.description: Analytics in test environments need care to avoid polluting production data. Use debug mode or separate GA properties for testing.

questions.question: What browsers must be supported (e.g., Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge)? Any specific versions?
questions.description: Browser support matrix directly affects what CSS, JS, and Web APIs can be used without polyfills.

questions.question: Are we supporting legacy browsers (e.g., IE11) or only evergreen browsers?
questions.description: IE11 support adds significant overhead (no CSS Grid, no flexbox gap, no ES modules). It's worth explicitly confirming if needed.

questions.question: What devices and screen sizes must the UI accommodate (desktop, mobile, tablet)?
questions.description: Mobile-first development is standard. Knowing target devices early helps define breakpoints and touch interaction patterns.

questions.question: Are responsive breakpoints and layouts specified (e.g., mobile < 480px, tablet 768px+, etc.)?
questions.description: Defined breakpoints prevent inconsistent responsive behavior. Common breakpoints: 320, 480, 768, 1024, 1280, 1440px.

questions.question: Has the design been tested for mobile-friendliness? Over 70% of users browse on mobile[4], so responsive design is critical.
questions.description: Mobile traffic often exceeds 70% for consumer-facing apps. Test on real devices, not just browser DevTools emulation.

questions.question: For mobile support: is the site optimized for touch interactions and smaller screens?
questions.description: Touch targets should be at least 44×44px (Apple HIG) to prevent mis-taps. Hover-only interactions don't work on touch screens.

questions.question: Are there platform-specific design considerations (e.g., mobile app styles, iOS vs Android navigation)?
questions.description: iOS and Android have different navigation conventions (back button behavior, safe areas, font rendering). Design for both explicitly.

questions.question: How should the UI behave if offline or if network is slow (offline indicators, cached content)?
questions.description: Offline states should show a clear message rather than a broken UI. Service workers can cache critical resources for offline use.

questions.question: Which analytics or user-tracking tools will be used (Google Analytics, GA4, Hotjar, etc.)?
questions.description: Different tools serve different purposes: GA4 for traffic, Hotjar for heatmaps, Mixpanel for product analytics. Confirm which are needed.

questions.question: Who will provide tracking IDs or tags?
questions.description: Tracking IDs must be environment-specific (dev/staging/prod). Sharing production IDs across environments pollutes analytics data.

questions.question: What specific events or conversions need to be tracked (e.g., clicks, sign-ups, purchases)?
questions.description: Event taxonomy should be agreed upfront. Inconsistent event naming across features makes data analysis difficult.

questions.question: Will custom dimensions/metrics or e-commerce tracking be needed in analytics?
questions.description: E-commerce tracking (enhanced ecommerce, conversion funnels) requires specific dataLayer pushes that must be planned in the component design.

questions.question: How will we test and validate that tracking is correctly firing?
questions.description: Use GTM's Preview mode or browser extensions like GA Debugger to verify tracking fires correctly before launch.

questions.question: Are there dashboards or reports already set up for this project’s analytics?
questions.description: Existing dashboards clarify what data is already being captured and what gaps need to be filled.

questions.question: What languages and locales must the site/app support? (More than half of users prefer content in their native language[5].)
questions.description: i18n architecture (React-Intl, next-i18next, etc.) is very hard to retrofit. It must be planned from the start if multiple locales are needed.

questions.question: How will translations be managed and updated (gettext files, JSON, translation service)?
questions.description: JSON translation files work well for small projects. Larger projects need a TMS (translation management system) like Phrase or Lokalise.

questions.question: Are there right-to-left (RTL) language requirements? How should layouts adapt?
questions.description: RTL support requires layout mirroring. CSS logical properties (margin-inline-start instead of margin-left) simplify RTL handling.

questions.question: Are date, time, number, and currency formats different by locale?
questions.description: date-fns or Intl.DateTimeFormat handle locale-specific formatting. Currency and number formats also vary significantly by locale.

questions.question: Will switching languages require a page reload, or should it be dynamic?
questions.description: Dynamic locale switching without reload requires all text to come from the i18n layer — hardcoded strings break it.

questions.question: Who will provide translated copy, and what is the workflow for updating it?
questions.description: Translation workflows need coordination between developers, content managers, and translators. Missing translations should show fallback text, not break the UI.

questions.question: What environments exist (development, staging, production)? Who has access to each?
questions.description: Environment parity reduces "works on my machine" issues. Know who can deploy to each environment and what access is needed.

questions.question: What is the version control strategy (Git repo URL, branching model, CI/CD pipeline)?
questions.description: CI/CD pipelines prevent manual deployment errors. Define the branching strategy (GitFlow, trunk-based) and who approves merges.

questions.question: Where will the app be hosted (cloud provider, on-prem)? Will Docker or other containers be used?
questions.description: Hosting choice affects config: Vercel for Next.js, AWS for custom, Docker for containerized. Each has different deployment workflows.

questions.question: Who manages DNS, SSL certificates, and domains?
questions.description: DNS changes can take up to 48h to propagate. SSL provisioning via Let's Encrypt or ACM needs to happen before go-live.

questions.question: How often will new releases be deployed, and what is the process (manual or automated)?
questions.description: Automated deployments on merge to main are the gold standard. Manual deployments introduce human error and deployment anxiety.

questions.question: How will rollbacks or hotfixes be handled if something goes wrong?
questions.description: A rollback plan is essential. Whether that's reverting a deploy, feature-flagging off a bad release, or hotfixing, define the process upfront.

questions.question: For ongoing maintenance, who will support the app post-launch? Who do we contact with issues?
questions.description: Post-launch support ownership is often unclear. Define SLAs, communication channels, and who triages production issues.

questions.question: If migrating: Will the old and new systems run in parallel? How and when will cutover happen?
questions.description: Running old and new systems in parallel (strangler-fig pattern) reduces risk but requires traffic routing and session handling between systems.

questions.question: What is the project timeline or target launch date? Are there any hard deadlines?
questions.description: Hard deadlines (product launches, trade shows, contractual dates) constrain scope. Know them before estimating.

questions.question: Are there major milestones (e.g., design freeze, feature complete) we should plan for?
questions.description: Milestones like design freeze prevent design changes after frontend development has started, which causes costly rework.

questions.question: What is the preferred communication channel (email, Slack, project management tool)?
questions.description: Misaligned communication channels create lost context. Agree on a single place for decisions and another for async updates.

questions.question: How frequently should status updates or demos be provided?
questions.description: Weekly demos keep stakeholders engaged and surface misalignments early, when they're cheap to fix.

questions.question: Who are the key stakeholders (product owner, marketing lead, QA) and decision-makers?
questions.description: When too many people have approval power, nothing gets approved. Identify one decision-maker per area.

questions.question: What is the expected turnaround time for feedback on questions or deliverables?
questions.description: Slow feedback loops are a leading cause of delays. A 48-hour SLA for feedback is a reasonable baseline.

questions.question: Roles & Responsibilities: Who is responsible for code quality, writing tests, and handling support/bug fixes[6]?
questions.description: Unclear ownership of code quality, testing, and bug fixes leads to finger-pointing. Write it down before the project starts.

questions.question: Are there internal coding standards or linters we must adhere to?
questions.description: ESLint, Prettier, and editor config (.editorconfig) enforce consistency. Agree on rules before writing the first line of code.

questions.question: How will requirements and changes be documented (spec sheets, tickets, user stories)?
questions.description: Living documentation in tickets/user stories is more useful than waterfall specs. Agree on the format and where it lives.

questions.question: Is there a process for change requests after development has started?
questions.description: Changes after development starts are expensive. A formal change request process (even lightweight) prevents scope creep.

questions.question: What user data will be collected by the app? How must it be protected?
questions.description: Data minimization is a GDPR principle: only collect what you need. Know what's collected to design appropriate consent flows and storage.

questions.question: Are there specific compliance requirements (GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, PCI) impacting the frontend?
questions.description: GDPR applies to EU users regardless of where the company is based. HIPAA applies to health data. PCI to payment data. Know which apply.

questions.question: How should sensitive data be handled on the client side (encrypt in storage, use httpOnly cookies)?
questions.description: Sensitive data in localStorage or sessionStorage is readable by any JS on the page. Use httpOnly cookies for auth tokens.

questions.question: What authentication flows are needed (login, logout, registration, password reset, MFA)?
questions.description: Auth flows (especially MFA, OAuth, and password reset) are complex and security-critical. Use a proven library rather than rolling your own.

questions.question: Are there security measures like CAPTCHA on forms or rate-limiting for login attempts?
questions.description: CAPTCHA (reCAPTCHA, Turnstile) and login rate limiting protect against brute-force attacks. These are backend concerns but affect frontend UX.

questions.question: How should security vulnerabilities be reported and addressed during development?
questions.description: A responsible disclosure process (security.txt, CVE reporting) lets researchers report vulnerabilities safely. Define this before launch.

questions.question: Is TypeScript required? What strictness level is expected (strict, noImplicitAny, etc.)?
questions.description: TypeScript strict mode catches many bugs early. Confirm the baseline config with the team.

questions.question: What state management approach is preferred? (Redux Toolkit, Zustand, Context API, Jotai, TanStack Query for server state)
questions.description: Misaligned state management choices cause rework. Agree before building the first component.

questions.question: Are there preferred UI component libraries? (shadcn/ui, MUI, Ant Design, Radix, or fully custom)
questions.description: Influences design system, bundle size, and theming strategy.

questions.question: What bundler/build tool is expected? (Webpack, Vite, Turbopack, Parcel)
questions.description: Affects dev server speed, config compatibility, and CI build times.

questions.question: Is there a monorepo setup? (Turborepo, Nx, plain npm/yarn workspaces)
questions.description: Monorepo structure affects how shared packages and apps are developed and deployed.

questions.question: Are there environment variables that must be exposed to the browser (NEXT_PUBLIC_*)? Who owns and manages .env files?
questions.description: Leaked secrets and missing vars are common deployment blockers.

questions.question: What minimum code/test coverage percentage is expected? Which testing frameworks? (Jest, Vitest, Cypress, Playwright)
questions.description: Setting coverage expectations early avoids last-minute scrambles before release.

questions.question: Is there a design token / style dictionary pipeline already in place? Should tokens be auto-synced from Figma?
questions.description: Determines whether CSS variables should come from a generated token file or be written manually.

questions.question: What font family and weight are used for each text style, and what fallback fonts should be applied?
questions.description: Font fallbacks prevent layout shifts when custom fonts fail to load. System font stacks (Georgia, Arial) ensure readable text in all conditions.

questions.question: What are the font sizes for headings, subheadings, body text, captions, etc.? Are they fixed or responsive (e.g. using rem or viewport units)?
questions.description: Responsive font sizes using clamp() or viewport units scale smoothly across screen sizes without breakpoint-specific overrides.

questions.question: What are the exact line-height and letter-spacing values for each text style?
questions.description: Line height affects readability. Body text typically needs 1.4–1.6 line height. Too tight causes eye fatigue; too loose breaks visual grouping.

questions.question: Are any text-transformations applied (e.g. uppercase, small-caps, caps)?
questions.description: CSS text-transform (uppercase, capitalize) should be applied via CSS, not hardcoded in HTML, so text remains copyable and translatable.

questions.question: Should a given text style map to a specific HTML element (e.g. this title as <H1> vs <H2>)?[2]
questions.description: HTML heading hierarchy (h1 → h6) affects SEO and accessibility. The visual style and the semantic level should not be conflated.

questions.question: Are the text styles based on design-system tokens? If custom, why not use an existing token?
questions.description: Design tokens for typography ensure consistency. Custom values that aren't tokenized tend to proliferate and cause inconsistency over time.

questions.question: Are placeholder (dummy) text or content lengths considered (for translations or dynamic content)? If not, how to handle overflow/truncation? If text is overflowing how should we break it?
questions.description: Long translated text often exceeds the original English by 30–40%. Components must handle text overflow gracefully with ellipsis or wrapping.

questions.question: What are the exact color values (hex/RGBA) for all UI elements (text, backgrounds, borders, icons)? Are these defined as design tokens or CSS variables?[1]
questions.description: CSS custom properties (variables) make theme changes trivial. Hardcoded hex values throughout the codebase make theming a nightmare.

questions.question: Which colors correspond to semantic roles (primary, secondary, success, warning, error, background, surface, etc.)?
questions.description: Semantic color roles (primary, error, surface) decouple meaning from specific values. This is the foundation for theming and dark mode.

questions.question: Are light/dark mode (or high-contrast) variants defined? If so, what changes (colors, overlays, text colors) when switching themes?
questions.description: Dark mode requires more than inverting colors. Background hierarchy, shadow visibility, and image brightness all need redesign.

questions.question: Are hover/active color variants specified for buttons, links, icons, etc.?
questions.description: Interactive states (hover, active, focus) are critical for usability. If they're not in the design, developers will guess — often incorrectly.

questions.question: Have all text/background color combinations been checked for WCAG contrast compliance? If not, should alternate colors be provided?[6]
questions.description: WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 contrast for normal text and 3:1 for large text. Automated tools like axe-core catch most violations.

questions.question: Are any gradients, overlays, or shadows used? If so, what are the start/end colors, opacities, and directions?
questions.description: CSS gradients need specific angle, color stop positions, and browser prefixes. Vague descriptions ("blue to purple") lead to mismatches.

questions.question: Are transparent colors or alpha (opacity) values given for overlays or disabled states?
questions.description: RGBA colors and opacity need to be specified precisely. rgba(0,0,0,0.5) and a 50% opacity layer behave differently in compositing.

questions.question: What grid or column system is intended (e.g. 12-column grid, fixed-width container)?
questions.description: A 12-column grid maps naturally to common screen proportions and allows 1, 2, 3, 4, and 6-column layouts without breaking the grid.

questions.question: What are the precise margins, padding, and gap values between elements? Are they multiples of a base spacing unit? Should they scale or remain fixed?[7]
questions.description: An 8px base spacing unit creates visual harmony. All margins, padding, and gaps should be multiples of this unit for consistency.

questions.question: Are elements full-width, centered, or fixed-width? For example, what is the maximum container width on desktop?
questions.description: Max-width containers (typically 1200–1440px) prevent content from becoming too wide on large screens. Center-align with horizontal padding.

questions.question: For 1px borders or hairline strokes: is this supposed to be 1 device pixel on all screens (e.g. a retina hairline) or a fixed CSS pixel?
questions.description: On retina screens, a CSS 1px border renders as 2 physical pixels. True hairlines require 0.5px or transform tricks on iOS.

questions.question: Are any elements positioned absolutely or using CSS transforms (e.g. overlays, modals)? If so, how are their positions defined?
questions.description: Absolutely positioned overlays and modals need z-index management. Establish a z-index scale early to prevent stacking order conflicts.

questions.question: Should all spacing adhere to a grid or design-token (e.g. 8px, 16px)? If custom values are used, what is the rationale?
questions.description: Off-grid custom values are technical debt. If a designer uses 13px padding, ask if 12px (or 16px) achieves the same visual result.

questions.question: Should hidden elements (e.g. off-screen menus) have any specific spacing or is hidden by default?
questions.description: Off-screen elements (drawers, dropdowns) should be truly hidden (display:none or inert attribute) to prevent screen readers from reading hidden content.

questions.question: What screen sizes (mobile, tablet, desktop) are supported? Are explicit breakpoints defined (e.g. 320px, 768px, 1024px)?
questions.description: Mobile-first breakpoints (min-width) are preferable to desktop-down (max-width) as they build complexity incrementally.

questions.question: Are alternate layouts or mockups provided for smaller screens, or is the design meant to be fluid? How do elements reflow or resize on narrow viewports?
questions.description: Fluid layouts using percentages and max-width are lower maintenance than breakpoint-heavy designs. Confirm the approach upfront.

questions.question: At what width do navigation or multi-column layouts collapse (e.g. switch to hamburger menu or single column)?
questions.description: Navigation is the most impactful responsive change. Hamburger menus need animation specs, overlay behavior, and focus management.

questions.question: Should font sizes or spacing change at certain breakpoints (e.g. larger text on mobile)?
questions.description: Fluid type scaling with clamp(min, preferred, max) eliminates most breakpoint-based font size overrides.

questions.question: What is the minimum/maximum width or height for this layout or its images? (e.g. if an image scales, what are its min/max dimensions?)[4]
questions.description: Images without size constraints can stretch or squish unpredictably. Use the aspect-ratio CSS property to maintain proportions.

questions.question: Are device safe-area insets (e.g. notch, status bar) considered on mobile? (E.g. top padding on iOS, Android navigation bar.)
questions.description: iOS Safari adds safe area insets for the notch and home indicator. Use env(safe-area-inset-*) CSS variables to avoid overlap.

questions.question: Are any touch-specific behaviors required on mobile (e.g. swipe to open menu)? How should hover-only interactions be handled on touch devices?
questions.description: Touch-only interactions (swipe, long-press) need explicit fallbacks for mouse/keyboard users. Pointer events API can distinguish input types.

questions.question: For each component (buttons, inputs, cards, etc.), are all states provided: default, hover, focus, active/pressed, disabled, and loading?[8][3]
questions.description: Missing states are the most common design-to-development gap. A component without hover/focus/disabled states is incomplete.

questions.question: What is the visual style for each state? (e.g. button hover color, pressed inset, input focus outline, disabled opacity.)
questions.description: Visual state specifications prevent guesswork. "Slightly darker on hover" is ambiguous — specify the exact color or darken percentage.

questions.question: How should empty or placeholder states look? (e.g. empty table, no search results, blank form state)
questions.description: Empty states are a UX opportunity. A thoughtful empty state (illustration + call-to-action) is better than a blank area.

questions.question: How should error states appear? (e.g. form validation errors, error messages, styles for invalid fields)
questions.description: Inline validation (per-field) vs. summary errors (on submit) are different UX patterns. Decide which to use and be consistent.

questions.question: What does a “success” or confirmation state look like (if applicable)?
questions.description: Success states (confirmation toasts, green checkmarks) complete the user's mental model of an action. Don't skip them.

questions.question: Do interactive elements have defined focus indicators? For example, what outline or shadow shows when a button or link is focused? (Often covered with hover/focus specs)
questions.description: Focus indicators are mandatory for keyboard accessibility. The default browser outline is often removed by CSS resets and must be replaced.

questions.question: If a component toggles (e.g. play/pause, open/closed accordion), are both states in the design?
questions.description: Toggle components must visually distinguish their two states. Relying only on position (e.g., a switch) can be unclear without a label.

questions.question: If a component shows loading (spinner or skeleton), what color and size should it be?
questions.description: Skeleton loaders (grey placeholder shapes) reduce perceived load time better than spinners for content-heavy layouts.

questions.question: Are these design values (colors, spacing, font sizes, etc.) from an existing design-token library? If custom values are used, should they be added to the token set?[9]
questions.description: Design tokens as the source of truth (Figma tokens → Style Dictionary → CSS variables) enable design-dev sync without manual translation.

questions.question: Should these values be implemented as CSS custom properties (variables)? Which ones correspond to light/dark mode tokens or theme variables?
questions.description: CSS custom properties scoped to :root are the simplest way to implement tokens. Theme switching becomes a class toggle on the html element.

questions.question: Are there any unit choices to clarify (px vs rem vs %)? (For example, if spacing uses 16px, should we use 1rem=16px or keep px?)
questions.description: rem-based sizing scales with user browser font preferences (accessibility). px values are fixed and ignore user preferences.

questions.question: Are measurements given in the design final (or will they change)? If the designer changes a token (e.g. primary color) later, how will that be communicated?
questions.description: Versioned design tokens with a changelog prevent silent breakages. Communicate token changes through the same channel as code changes.

questions.question: What file formats should we use (SVG for icons, PNG/JPEG for raster)? Are SVGs provided for vector art?[10]
questions.description: SVG icons scale infinitely, support currentColor, and add no HTTP requests when inlined. Prefer SVG over icon fonts or raster for UI icons.

questions.question: What are the required resolutions of raster images (1x, 2x, 3x)? For example, should we export @2x images for retina?[10]
questions.description: Retina displays (@2x, @3x) require higher-resolution images. Use srcset or CSS image-set to serve the right size per device.

questions.question: What is the intended min/max display size of each image/icon? (e.g. should this image scale down to 100px wide or never shrink below 200px?)[4]
questions.description: Images without size constraints cause layout shifts (Cumulative Layout Shift). Set explicit width and height attributes to reserve space.

questions.question: Should icons use currentColor (to inherit text color) or fixed fill colors? Are multi-color icons final?
questions.description: currentColor SVGs inherit the parent's text color, making them easy to theme. Multi-color icons need explicit fill values.

questions.question: Are all icons and images organized in shared folders or libraries? (Designers often supply a zip of exported assets or keep them in Figma)[11]
questions.description: A consistent asset delivery method (Figma export, shared design system package, or CDN folder) prevents confusion about which version to use.

questions.question: For logos/illustrations, should they have transparent background or a fixed color? Are source files (AI, PSD) needed or is SVG sufficient?
questions.description: Logos on transparent backgrounds work on any background color. White-background logos require a specific background context to display correctly.

questions.question: If an image is purely decorative, should it have an empty alt (aria-hidden)? For meaningful images, what alt text should be used?[5]
questions.description: Purely decorative images (backgrounds, flourishes) should use aria-hidden="true" or empty alt. Informational images need descriptive alt text.

questions.question: What should happen on user interactions? (For example, does clicking this button open a modal, navigate to a page, submit a form, Scroll etc.?)
questions.description: Interaction specs prevent frontend developers from guessing behavior. "Clicking this button does X" is the minimum useful spec.

questions.question: Should tooltips, dropdowns or popovers close when clicking outside or pressing ESC?
questions.description: Click-outside-to-close is standard UX for dropdowns and modals. Confirm it's expected and whether ESC also closes the element.

questions.question: After an action, what is the resulting state? (E.g. if there is a play button, should it switch to a pause icon upon click?)[12]
questions.description: Toggled state changes (play → pause, like → liked) need both states designed. The active state is often forgotten.

questions.question: Are animations or transitions specified? If so, what easing and duration should be used (e.g. fade in 300ms ease-in-out)?
questions.description: Animation timing: 150–300ms feels snappy, 300–500ms feels smooth, 500ms+ feels slow. Use ease-out for entrances, ease-in for exits.

questions.question: If elements slide or scroll (e.g. carousels, accordions), should they auto-play or wait for user action?
questions.description: Auto-playing carousels distract users and fail accessibility guidelines. If auto-play is required, include a pause control.

questions.question: Are there loading animations (e.g. spinners)? What speed/color are they?
questions.description: Loading spinners should inherit the brand color and have a consistent size. Specify the animation speed (0.8–1.2s per rotation).

questions.question: For touch interfaces, are there swipe or gesture interactions?
questions.description: Touch gestures (swipe, pinch) need threshold values to avoid accidental triggers. They must not conflict with system-level gestures.

questions.question: Are appropriate alt text or ARIA labels provided for all non-text content (icons, images)? If an icon is purely decorative, should it be hidden from screen readers?[5]
questions.description: Decorative icons should have aria-hidden="true" to avoid cluttering screen reader output. Functional icons need an aria-label.

questions.question: Are all form controls and buttons labeled (e.g. using <label> or aria-label)?
questions.description: Visible labels are preferred over placeholder text (which disappears on input). Icon-only buttons need aria-label for screen readers.

questions.question: Are focus styles defined for all interactive elements (so keyboard users can see focus)?[3]
questions.description: Custom focus styles should be at least as visible as the default browser outline. 2px solid outline with 2px offset is a reliable baseline.

questions.question: Do all color contrasts meet WCAG 2.1 AA standards? (If not, what adjustments or alternatives exist?)[6]
questions.description: Use a contrast checker tool (axe, Lighthouse, or the browser devtools accessibility panel) to verify all text meets WCAG AA standards.

questions.question: Should any dynamic content (like alerts or modals) announce itself to assistive tech (e.g. role="alert")?
questions.description: role="alert" and aria-live="polite" let screen readers announce dynamic content changes. Critical for form errors and toast notifications.

questions.question: Are animations used in a way that could trigger motion sickness? Should prefers-reduced-motion be supported (or provide a non-animated alternative)?
questions.description: Parallax, looping animations, and flashing content can trigger vestibular disorders. Always support the prefers-reduced-motion media query.

questions.question: Is RTL (right-to-left) support needed (layout flip, mirrored icons)?
questions.description: RTL support requires layout mirroring for most UI elements (text, icons, navigation flow) but not all (phone numbers, prices, media controls).

questions.question: Who will handle exporting assets: designer or developer? If designer provides assets, should they supply a ZIP of images/icons or keep them in Figma?[11]
questions.description: A clear asset handoff process prevents version confusion. Figma exports → shared drive, versioned with component names, is a reliable workflow.

questions.question: What formats and resolutions should be exported for each asset? (E.g. SVG for icons; PNG @1x,2x for photos; WebP for web images)[10]
questions.description: WebP with JPEG/PNG fallback is the modern standard. SVG for icons and illustrations. AVIF for maximum compression where supported.

questions.question: Are font files needed? (If custom fonts are used, how should dev obtain/embed them—Google Fonts link, purchased files, etc.?)
questions.description: Custom fonts need licensing for web use. Google Fonts is free; commercial fonts may require a web license separate from the desktop license.

questions.question: Are images going to be breakpoint specific? Will the image change after certain breakpoints?
questions.description: Art-directed responsive images (different crops per breakpoint) use the <picture> element with multiple source elements.

questions.question: How should exported assets be named and organized? (E.g. icon-upload.svg, bg-hero-2x.jpg, etc.)
questions.description: Consistent asset naming (kebab-case, descriptive) prevents confusion. icon-arrow-right.svg is clearer than arrow2-final-v3.svg.

questions.question: If using icon sprites or fonts (e.g. FontAwesome), which style/version to use?
questions.description: FontAwesome and similar icon libraries add significant bundle size if not tree-shaken. SVG sprites or individual imports are more efficient.

questions.question: Are there any non-UI assets (videos, Lottie animations, PDFs) needed? Where are they?
questions.description: Lottie animations are JSON files that play in the browser. Videos need format choices (MP4/WebM) and autoplay policies clarified.

questions.question: Which platforms is this design targeting (Web, iOS, Android, Desktop)? Are any platform conventions required (e.g. iOS tab bar height, Android material button style)?
questions.description: Web, iOS, and Android each have platform conventions (navigation patterns, typography scales, interaction models) that users expect.

questions.question: For web vs native: Are there differences in navigation or UI patterns we should follow? (e.g. swipe gestures on mobile vs click on desktop)
questions.description: Bottom navigation is standard on mobile (iOS/Android); top navigation is standard on desktop. Users notice when conventions are violated.

questions.question: Are there any known browser or OS limitations (e.g. older IE, low-end Android) that affect implementation?
questions.description: Legacy browser limitations (older Chromium on Android, Safari on older iOS) often surprise developers who only test on latest Chrome.

questions.question: On mobile, should touch targets meet a minimum size (e.g. 44pt for iOS) – are all tappable areas large enough?
questions.description: 44×44pt touch targets (Apple HIG) and 48×48dp (Material Design) prevent accidental taps on small screens.

questions.question: If this is a desktop app (Electron, etc.), how should window resizing or DPI scaling be handled?
questions.description: Electron apps need to account for system window controls, DPI scaling (especially on Windows), and menu bar integration.

questions.question: Should we include platform-specific assets (e.g. splash screens, app icons of various sizes)?
questions.description: App store icons need multiple sizes. Generating them from a single SVG source is the most efficient approach.

questions.question: After implementation, are all typography, color, and spacing values pixel-perfect as per design?
questions.description: Pixel-perfect implementation requires a side-by-side comparison tool. Browser extensions like PixelParallel or Figma's inspect panel help verify exact values.

questions.question: Have all component states and interactions (hover/focus/active/loading) been implemented and tested?
questions.description: State coverage in QA: manually test hover/focus/active/loading/error/success for every interactive component before marking it done.

questions.question: Are all images/icons correct (no placeholders), optimized, and in the right format/resolution?
questions.description: Placeholder images in production are a common oversight. Search for common placeholder services (via.placeholder.com, picsum.photos) before launch.

questions.question: Have you verified accessibility requirements (alt text, contrast, focus states) have been met?[6][3]
questions.description: Run axe DevTools or Lighthouse accessibility audit as part of the definition of done — not as a last-minute check.

questions.question: Is the correct (final) design file being used? If the design is updated, has the dev version been synced?
questions.description: Design files get updated during development. Always confirm you're building from the latest approved version, not an earlier draft.

questions.question: Are all user flows complete (including error and empty states)?
questions.description: Error and empty states are frequently skipped in QA. Test them explicitly: disconnect from the network, submit invalid data, clear all content.

questions.question: On each device and browser, does the layout match the design, including responsive breakpoints?
questions.description: Test on real devices, not just browser emulation. iOS Safari and Chrome on Android render differently from desktop Chrome DevTools.

questions.question: Are there any UI bugs (misaligned items, off-by-one pixels)? (For example: is a 1px border rendering as a hairline? Is text truncating properly?)
questions.description: UI bugs like misaligned items compound: one 1px error causes a cascade across the layout. Use browser zoom (125%, 150%) to surface subpixel issues.

questions.question: Are any translations/localizations complete? If not, do designs accommodate longer text (e.g. menu items, buttons)?
questions.description: German and Finnish translations can be 40–50% longer than English. Test all UI elements with the longest expected translation.

questions.question: Has the QA process checked for differences from the design (colors, fonts, spacing)? Any discrepancies should be clarified with the designer.
questions.description: Design-dev discrepancy review (redline comparison) catches inconsistencies that developers introduce unintentionally. Include it in the QA definition of done.

questions.question: Which API endpoints will the frontend need? (List all expected endpoints for data retrieval and submission.)
questions.description: A complete API inventory prevents frontend blockers. Without knowing what endpoints exist, frontend work cannot be properly estimated or started.

questions.question: Are the APIs RESTful or GraphQL? Are there example queries or schemas?
questions.description: REST and GraphQL have very different data-fetching patterns. GraphQL enables precise data fetching but requires a different caching strategy.

questions.question: Is API documentation available (OpenAPI/Swagger specs, Postman collections)? If not, who will provide it?
questions.description: API documentation is the contract between frontend and backend. OpenAPI specs enable code generation (openapi-typescript) and mock server creation.

questions.question: How will the front-end handle data caching or offline data (if needed)?
questions.description: Client-side caching strategies (React Query, SWR, Apollo) reduce redundant API calls. Decide the caching approach before building the data layer.

questions.question: How should we handle API versioning? Are multiple API versions to be supported concurrently?
questions.description: API versioning (v1/v2 in URL vs Accept header) affects how the frontend handles backward compatibility during migrations.

questions.question: What pagination or filtering parameters do the APIs use?
questions.description: Pagination patterns (offset-based, cursor-based) affect infinite scroll vs. numbered pages UI. Cursor-based pagination is more performant for large datasets.

questions.question: Are there any bulk data export/import endpoints or admin-specific APIs?
questions.description: Bulk export endpoints (CSV, Excel) are often needed for admin features. They have different performance characteristics than standard REST endpoints.

questions.question: What authentication mechanism is used (session cookies, JWT, OAuth2, SAML, etc.)?
questions.description: Auth mechanism determines the entire security architecture. JWT requires token refresh logic; session cookies need CSRF protection; OAuth needs redirect handling.

questions.question: Are there user roles or permissions that affect what data the frontend can fetch?
questions.description: Permissions affect what components render, what API calls succeed, and what routes are accessible. Role definitions must be finalized before building guards.

questions.question: How should the frontend obtain and store authentication tokens or cookies? (e.g., secure, httpOnly cookies)
questions.description: httpOnly cookies are inaccessible to JavaScript (preventing XSS theft). localStorage tokens are convenient but vulnerable to XSS attacks.

questions.question: Will there be Single Sign-On (SSO) integration? Which identity provider (Okta, Azure AD, etc.)?
questions.description: SSO dramatically simplifies user experience in enterprise apps. Each identity provider (Okta, Azure AD) has its own OAuth2/SAML implementation quirks.

questions.question: Are there multi-factor authentication or CAPTCHA requirements for login?
questions.description: CAPTCHA (reCAPTCHA v3, Cloudflare Turnstile) adds server-side verification to login. MFA (TOTP, SMS) adds a second authentication factor.

questions.question: How should password resets, email confirmations, and user registration be handled?
questions.description: Password reset flows require secure token generation, expiry handling, and email delivery. These are security-critical and should use proven libraries.

questions.question: What databases or storage systems are used (SQL, NoSQL, Elasticsearch, etc.)?
questions.description: Database technology affects query patterns and data structures. SQL schemas are rigid but well-suited to relational data; NoSQL is flexible but requires careful modeling.

questions.question: How does the CMS (AEM/Magento) serve content – via APIs or templated pages?
questions.description: AEM and Magento can serve content via REST/GraphQL APIs (headless) or render HTML pages (traditional). The approach fundamentally changes the frontend architecture.

questions.question: Are there specific caching layers (Redis, CDN, in-memory) to use for frequent data?
questions.description: Redis caching can reduce API response times from 200ms to 5ms for frequently accessed data. Know what's cached and what the invalidation strategy is.

questions.question: How are assets (images, videos) stored and served? (e.g., cloud storage, CDN, AEM DAM)
questions.description: Asset delivery via CDN (Cloudinary, Imgix, AWS CloudFront) provides automatic image optimization and global edge caching.

questions.question: Are there data synchronization jobs (e.g., importing products from ERP) that affect the frontend?
questions.description: ERP sync jobs can cause periods of data inconsistency. The frontend needs to handle stale data gracefully (loading states, refresh prompts).

questions.question: If product data changes frequently, how is cache invalidation handled?
questions.description: Cache invalidation is one of the hardest problems in computing. Understand the strategy (TTL, event-based, manual purge) and its implications for data freshness.

questions.question: What third-party services must be integrated (payment gateways, email services, analytics)?
questions.description: Third-party integrations (Stripe, SendGrid, Twilio) each have their own auth patterns, webhook setups, and error handling requirements.

questions.question: Will you provide API keys/credentials for those services?
questions.description: API keys and secrets must be stored in environment variables, never committed to the repository. Confirm who provisions them per environment.

questions.question: Are SDKs or libraries required for these integrations?
questions.description: Official SDKs abstract away API complexities but add bundle size. Evaluate whether raw API calls are sufficient for simple integrations.

questions.question: What rate limits or quotas exist on third-party APIs?
questions.description: Rate limits require exponential backoff retry logic on the frontend. Hitting limits in production causes user-visible errors.

questions.question: Are there any licensing or compliance considerations for third-party tools?
questions.description: GPL-licensed libraries in a commercial product can have legal implications. Confirm with legal if using any copyleft-licensed dependencies.

questions.question: Are npm or other libraries restricted (no license, security risk)?
questions.description: Internal security policies often ban packages with known CVEs or certain licenses. Check with the security team before introducing new dependencies.

questions.question: What format do backend errors use? (Consistent JSON structure with codes and messages?)
questions.description: A consistent error response structure (code, message, details) makes frontend error handling generic rather than per-endpoint custom logic.

questions.question: How should the front-end display different classes of errors (validation errors, server errors, network errors)?
questions.description: Error classification matters for UX: validation errors (user's fault) show inline; server errors (our fault) show a generic message with a retry option.

questions.question: Will there be a global error boundary or error page design to use?
questions.description: React Error Boundaries catch render errors but not async errors. A global error page design handles uncaught exceptions gracefully.

questions.question: Which logging/monitoring tools are used (Sentry, LogRocket, ELK, etc.)?
questions.description: Sentry provides real-time error tracking with stack traces and user context. Configure source maps to get meaningful line numbers in minified production code.

questions.question: How will frontend errors be reported back (are frontend errors logged)?
questions.description: Frontend errors (JS exceptions, network failures) should be logged to the same observability platform as backend errors for correlated debugging.

questions.question: In case of critical failures, who should be alerted and how?
questions.description: On-call escalation policies and incident runbooks should exist before launch. A production outage is not the time to figure out who to call.

questions.question: If migrating: What known bugs or error conditions exist that we should anticipate?
questions.description: Known bugs in the legacy system are likely to resurface during migration. Document them upfront to distinguish "new bug" from "existing behavior".

questions.question: What are the expected traffic patterns (peak users, API call volumes)?
questions.description: Traffic patterns (steady vs. bursty, peak hours) inform caching strategies and auto-scaling configuration.

questions.question: Will we need load balancing or auto-scaling configurations?
questions.description: Load balancing configuration affects sticky sessions. If sessions aren't shared across instances, users get logged out on load balancer failover.

questions.question: How are heavy tasks handled (batch jobs, on-demand scaling)?
questions.description: Heavy batch jobs running during peak hours degrade API performance. Schedule them during off-peak windows or use background queues.

questions.question: Are there SLAs for response times or uptime?
questions.description: SLAs define acceptable downtime and response time thresholds. These drive decisions about redundancy, failover, and monitoring alert thresholds.

questions.question: Will the API support gzip or other compression for responses?
questions.description: API response compression (gzip, Brotli) reduces bandwidth for large payloads. Most modern servers support it with a single config line.

questions.question: How should we handle large datasets (lazy loading, infinite scroll)?
questions.description: Virtual scrolling renders only visible rows in large lists, reducing DOM nodes from thousands to dozens. TanStack Virtual implements this pattern.

questions.question: What CORS policies are in place? Do we need to whitelist domains?
questions.description: CORS misconfiguration is a common security vulnerability. Allowlist specific origins rather than using wildcard (*) in production.

questions.question: What content security policy (CSP) or HTTP headers will the backend enforce?
questions.description: Content Security Policy (CSP) headers prevent XSS by whitelisting allowed script sources. Report-only mode lets you test without breaking anything.

questions.question: Are inputs validated and sanitized server-side (to prevent XSS, SQL injection)?
questions.description: Server-side validation is mandatory regardless of client-side validation. Never trust user input.

questions.question: Will the frontend need to integrate with CAPTCHA or other anti-bot measures?
questions.description: Google reCAPTCHA v3 works invisibly (no puzzle) but requires backend score evaluation. Cloudflare Turnstile is a privacy-respecting alternative.

questions.question: How are security headers (HSTS, X-Frame-Options, etc.) configured?
questions.description: HSTS forces HTTPS for a specified duration. X-Frame-Options (or CSP frame-ancestors) prevents clickjacking. These are standard baseline headers.

questions.question: Are there any audit or penetration test requirements for the frontend?
questions.description: Penetration tests typically find issues with input validation, authentication, and authorization logic. Schedule them before launch, not after.

questions.question: What environment variables or configuration (API endpoints, feature flags) will the front-end need?
questions.description: Frontend environment variables exposed via NEXT_PUBLIC_* are baked into the build. Never use them for secrets — they're visible in the browser source.

questions.question: Will the front-end code be deployed via a build pipeline (CI/CD)? Who triggers deployments?
questions.description: CI/CD on git push eliminates manual deployment steps and human error. GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Bitbucket Pipelines are common choices.

questions.question: Are there separate dev/staging databases or data mocks to use?
questions.description: Separate staging databases prevent test data from polluting production. Staging should mirror production schema but with anonymized data.

questions.question: Will we use containerization (Docker) for the front-end?
questions.description: Docker containers ensure environment parity between developer machines, CI, and production. Dockerfile and docker-compose.yml belong in the repo.

questions.question: What Node.js or build tool versions are standardized across environments?
questions.description: Locking Node.js versions in .nvmrc and CI config prevents subtle incompatibilities between developers and build servers.

questions.question: Are there linting and formatting rules enforced on the front-end code?
questions.description: ESLint + Prettier enforced in pre-commit hooks (via Husky + lint-staged) and CI catches style and quality issues before code review.

questions.question: What testing strategy is expected (unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests)?
questions.description: Unit tests verify logic, integration tests verify component interactions, E2E tests verify user flows. All three layers are needed for a robust suite.

questions.question: Are there automated test suites already in place? Which frameworks (Jest, Cypress)?
questions.description: Existing test suites reveal what's already covered and what patterns to follow. If there are no tests, agree on the minimum coverage before starting.

questions.question: Is there test data or staging data available for the front-end?
questions.description: Consistent test data prevents flaky tests. A seeded test database or MSW (Mock Service Worker) API mocks provide reliable, repeatable test conditions.

questions.question: Who is responsible for QA and how are defects managed (Jira, Bugzilla)?
questions.description: A defined defect lifecycle (discovered → assigned → fixed → verified → closed) prevents bugs from getting lost or duplicated.

questions.question: Are acceptance criteria defined for user stories?
questions.description: Acceptance criteria define "done". Without them, features get reopened repeatedly based on subjective interpretation.

questions.question: How will cross-browser and cross-device testing be handled?
questions.description: BrowserStack or LambdaTest provide cloud device farms for cross-browser/device testing without maintaining a physical device lab.

questions.question: Are load/performance tests planned for critical workflows?
questions.description: Load tests simulate concurrent users to find bottlenecks before launch. k6, Locust, or JMeter are common tools. Test against production-like data volumes.

questions.question: Where is project documentation maintained (wiki, Confluence, code comments)?
questions.description: Confluence/Notion for decision records, README for setup instructions, and JSDoc/TSDoc in code for API documentation serve different audiences.

questions.question: What is the policy for documenting new APIs or front-end components?
questions.description: Storybook serves as living component documentation. API/endpoint changes should be documented in the same PR as the code change.

questions.question: How are breaking changes communicated to the team?
questions.description: Breaking changes (API shape changes, removed features) need advance notice. Semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH) communicates impact level.

questions.question: Will there be a changelog or release notes for users?
questions.description: User-facing changelogs help support teams answer "what changed?" questions. CHANGELOG.md tracking technical changes benefits internal teams.

questions.question: Are there code review processes in place? (e.g., pull request requirements)
questions.description: Required reviewers, minimum approvals, and automated checks (CI green, no conflicts) define code review quality gates.

questions.question: If this is a migrated project: What is the current backend stack (versions of AEM/Magento, languages)?
questions.description: The current stack's versions determine what APIs, patterns, and constraints exist. AEM 6.5 vs AEM as Cloud Service have fundamentally different component models.

questions.question: What legacy data needs migration, and are there data transformation scripts planned?
questions.description: Data transformation scripts (ETL) for migration need testing with production-like data volumes. Data quality issues in the old system often surface here.

questions.question: Which parts of the old system are being deprecated?
questions.description: Documenting deprecated features prevents them from being accidentally re-implemented. A deprecation notice with a migration path helps the team.

questions.question: Are there legacy APIs we must support during transition?
questions.description: Legacy API compatibility during transition (strangler-fig pattern) allows gradual migration without a big-bang cutover.

questions.question: Who owns the old codebase and can answer questions about its functionality?
questions.description: The original developers are invaluable resources for undocumented behavior and tribal knowledge. Identify them early before they move on.

questions.question: How will we ensure feature parity (or note differences) with the existing system?
questions.description: Feature parity testing requires a side-by-side comparison checklist. Automated regression tests against the old system capture behavior before it's deleted.
